title: Fitzpatrick's Maiden Glory: Brother Matt Watches from Afar
excerpt: Alex Fitzpatrick captured his first DP World Tour title at the Indian Open, overcoming a four-stroke deficit to edge defending champion Eugenio Chacarra by two shots.
body:
The Fitzpatrick Breakthrough

Alex Fitzpatrick did what many thought impossible: he tracked down Eugenio Chacarra on Sunday at the Hero Indian Open to claim his maiden DP World Tour title. The 27-year-old Englishman trailed the defending champion by four strokes heading into the final round at DLF Golf and Country Club in Gurugram, Haryana — a commanding margin in professional golf. Yet Fitzpatrick kept his nerve while Chacarra faltered, posting a composed three-under 69 against the Spaniard's disastrous three-over 75 to seal a two-shot victory at nine-under 279.

Winning Score
-9
Margin
2 shots
Winner's Prize
€375,811
Race to Dubai Points
585

The Context: A Growing Fitzpatrick Empire

None of this happens in a vacuum. Alex's older brother Matt, a three-time PGA Tour winner and former US Open champion, won the Valspar Championship last week to climb to a career-high world No. 6 ranking. The Fitzpatricks are having a moment. "I idolise him, so just trying to be like him in every way," Alex told the crowd after his triumph. "I'm off to see them in a couple of days — I see my mum and dad tomorrow and we're going to meet my brother in Florida."

Chacarra's Sunday Collapse

For three rounds, Chacarra — the 2025 Indian Open champion — looked untouchable. He led by four shots entering Sunday's final 18 holes on the demanding DLF layout. But scoring conditions deteriorated, and Chacarra's ball-striking abandoned him when it mattered most. The Spaniard shot 75, squandering his position and handing the title to a hungrier challenger. Chacarra still banked €243,172 for second place, but in professional sport, second-place money is often cold comfort.

The Supporting Cast

Ugo Coussaud, MJ Daffue and Andy Sullivan all tied for third on five-under, with Francesco Molinari — the three-time Ryder Cup winner — finishing tied sixth alongside Ewen Ferguson and Calum Hill at three-under. The tournament delivered a quality field and a quality champion.

What's Next

With the Masters just two weeks away, Fitzpatrick's confidence will soar. He now owns 17 Official World Golf Ranking points and 585 Race to Dubai points. The Indian Open may lack the prestige of the majors, but a maiden DP World Tour title carries genuine weight — and it's precisely the sort of breakthrough moment that breeds further success. Expect Alex Fitzpatrick to make a strong showing at Augusta.
